speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that you got married and had children. How do you think your experiences have affected the way you brought up your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't know. I don't know. Who can tell? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When we lived in Israel, Israel was going through a very dynamic changes of its own. And we, of course, were caught up in the stream of life. Whatever happened before was totally forgotten. There was no time for that. 1948 was the war of independence. There were hardships and exhilaration of the independence of the state of Israel. And nobody had really the luxury at the time to stand by or to ruminate about the past. We were just swept by the stream of life in Israel. I went back to school. And I worked as a social worker in 1951. My first daughter was born in 1953. My second daughter was born. And we just lived a full life, just like anybody else. In 1958, we immigrated to the states. In 1960, we came to Canada. Both in the States and in Canada I was working. I was a full time worker and mother, a wife. We were both working very hard. And whatever was, was. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We didn't particularly hide from the children that I am a survivor. They knew my number. They lived with this. We used to drive them to a very [NON-ENGLISH], which they were reluctant to go to because there were just a few old people. They weren't interested, particularly. But through the years, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they grew with it. My sister and law was in Auschwitz, too. She has a number, too. They had very close contact with her. It was a part of reality. It was nothing that was over emphasized or under emphasized. Nevertheless, I am sure that they knew perhaps subconsciously not to ask too much. I don't know. It's very hard to tell you. All I can tell you is that they're both very supportive. They're both very Jewish. That they went with me to Auschwitz. That they were with me when there was a monument commemorating the former events in Israel always beside me. Both of them were quite active. Actually, one of them started the second generation in Ottawa with the first taping. So I don't know how to answer you.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned a commemoration in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that come about?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 19-- I believe it was 1982 or 1988. I'm not sure which. I corresponded with Marta. I correspond until this day. Marta was telling me that she has read a great deal about writings about [NON-ENGLISH] and her role in the resistance in Auschwitz. And she was telling me that high time has come to mention the three other girls who were forgotten by history. I couldn't be bothered. I was too busy. I was working. I just didn't want to. In 1988, Marta came to Canada with the express reason to tell me and to order me, get up and do. So I did. After all, I owe her my life. Whatever Marta said, I had to do. And since 1988, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we started the air force. First of all, there was a Mr. Ernst Kuhn, a survivor from Auschwitz, who has discovered that the bicycle factory in Frondenberg, in Germany belongs to the same people and is directed by the same people who directed the union factory in Auschwitz. He decided to bring them to court and demand reparation for the workers of the union factory. He erected the plaque for the four girls in Frondenberg in the cemetery. Unfortunately, he died without accomplishing his task. After that event, there started to be an organization called the Union Survivor's Committee in Israel, who felt that since there is a plaque in Germany, there also should be something in Yad Vashem to recognize the four girls. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They worked very, very hard for a number of years until finally succeeding in erecting a monument for the four girls in the commemorative gardens in Yad Vashem in Jerusalem. Following that, efforts were made by the same group with the chair person by the name of Lidia Vago to have the Auschwitz Museum recognize the four girls. And through their hard work, in 1994, a plaque commemorating the four Jewish heroines has been erected on the block 27 in Auschwitz. A block which is commemorated to the Jewish martyrdom during the Second World War. Following that, the Polish government has-- is honoring the four girls by giving them posthumously the Order of Valour, of Polish Valour.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that will be when?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this will be in March 21st, 1996 in the Polish embassy in Ottawa. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Following that, efforts are being made to correct the exhibit in the Memorial Museum in Washington, which [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so far is exhibiting only one picture instead of all four. They do cover the event. But they do not cover it by their exhibit. And efforts are being made to have them corrected.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you could talk to the younger generation today, what message would you want to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I will tell you why. I'm very confused. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] There is no question in my mind that when you meet with adversity of life, you have to stand tall. And you have to do what's right, because you can't divorce yourself from yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the same token, I paid too big a price. Perhaps if we weren't so right, my sister would be still alive. What was surprising for what? Did the world change? The world is not going to change unless we believe in changing the world, but we have to pay a very, very high price.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what to tell you. I don't know. I know that for me, the answer is to stand for what I believe in. I don't know if this is done for everybody. I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wouldn't tell this to the young people, but I'm telling this to you. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you been involved in educational programs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Since Marta came to talk to me, I didn't stop talking. I went with [NON-ENGLISH] in 1993 to Auschwitz. I spoke to the university students. I spoke in the March of the Living. I spoke in the video program that was made by the Chicago Holocaust community about resistance. I [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] am speaking occasionally in the schools in Ottawa to the young people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does it mean to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It tears me apart. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I do it because I want to believe that through the process of education, these young people will prevent the evil that was done, and can be done, and is being done. That by knowing to what extremes a totalitarian regime can bring, that they will stand up and be able to say no. And believe in themselves, and say there are certain things they are ready to do. And there are certain things that they will not allow to be done. I hope so.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like for you at the commemoration at Yad Vashem?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very spiritual experience. Marta was there. My sister, Sabina, was there. We were honored during the ceremony to light the eternal flame, both my sister and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as I bent down to light the flame, I saw both my parents smiling at me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we went back into the line, my sister bent over to me and said to me, do you know that our parents were here? So we both experienced the presence of our parents there. After the ceremony, I was talking to my younger daughter. And they told her, look, this is what happened. But they-- wait. I don't understand. Why didn't-- why wasn't Estusha there? And she said to me, why don't you understand? Her spirit is still alive. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this a drawing of?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1945, when I was in Belgium, I have written a diary about my war experiences. The first thing I did was this drawing of the outside of the union ammunition factory in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this a drawing of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the floor plan of the union factory in Auschwitz, which I draw in 1945. As far as I know, this is the only one in existence.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language is it in?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a combination of Polish and-- and German.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this a map of?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1995, I have translated the diary that I had written in Polish in 1945. And I have redone the floor plan of the union factory, translating the terms into English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you show us what the diagram is?

speaker: interviewee
text: The reason we are showing is to try and show you how the gunpowder was smuggled. I was working around here. Behind me was a glass wall. In this office was the glass cage elevated of the director of the factory, from which he had the bird's eye view on the whole factory. I used to walk from here, around under his office, through here to the gunpowder room where my sister worked. She used to give me the gun powder, tie it-- it was a string in little piece of material. I used to put it in a garbage metal box. Put the garbage-- another garbage box on top of it-- and walk right back through here, back into my seat right here. After a while, I used to take out the gun powder, go through here. It's a big corridor to the washrooms where I would share my gunpowder was another girl. And from there, on the way to home, to Birkenau with about three kilometers distance, we used to smuggle it into Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those are my parents, Jakob and Rebeka nee Wajcblum. This picture was taken, I think, about 1918.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this is a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture taken in 1920 is of Isabela Szapowska, my nanny.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture, taken in 1993, is of Sabina, Estusha, and Hanka. That's me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of Estusha taken in 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brzesc. She was visiting with our uncle in Brzesc [NON-ENGLISH]. It's in eastern part of Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture, taken in 1945, in Brussels, Belgium is of Marta and Hanka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is Marta?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Marta. And this is Hanka.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture, taken in the Israeli in Netanya in 1947 is of Hanka and Yehoshua Heilman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are this? Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of Ariella and Ray Faiola. Taken in 1981 in Larchmont, New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is the relationship with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Ariella is my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this is a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Jory Zev Faiola, my grandson. Born in 1982. And this picture is taken at his bar mitzvah in 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: This picture is of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is of my daughter, Noa, at her wedding day, 1984, with her husband Sheldon Schwartz. It's taken in Ottawa, Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: This picture is of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is of Jesse Milo Yishai Schwartz. My grandson, son of Noa and Sheldon. Born in 1984. This is a school picture taken in 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of a plaque commemorating the four heroines in Frondenberg, Germany. It was erected in 1985 through the efforts of a Mr. Ernst Kuhn, who found out that the directors of a factory of bicycles in Frondenberg were the same people who were directors of the union ammunition factory in Auschwitz. The translation of the plaque reads, 'We'll remember the Jewish citizens of Frondenberg, who were the victims of the Nazi violence. Will remember in sorrow the Jewish women and men who, as forced laborers, were worked to death in the Weichsel Metall Union Worker. We honor and bow to the four Jewish women who worked in Weichsel Metall Union Works commando at Auschwitz, and who were publicly hanged at the appellplatz in the presence of all the prisoners on the 6th of January, 1945. For us, those four women are the heroines of the Jewish resistance. And their fate must be a reminder to us to venerate life and never lose respect for the honor of others. And they are Ella Gartner, Roza Robota, Regina Safirsztain, and Ester Wajcblum.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my granddaughter, Danika Ester Eve Faiola, born in 1986. This picture is taken in New York in 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my grandson, Jacob Michael Schwartz, born in 1987. And this picture is a school picture taken in Regina in 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of a monument erected in honor of the four heroines of Auschwitz in 1991 in Yad Vashem in Jerusalem. Through the air force of the Union Survivors Committee, the chair woman being Lidia Vago.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of the exhibit in Jewish Holocaust Center, Victoria Australia taken in 1993 commemorating the four heroines of Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of a plaque honoring the four heroins. This plaque was erected by the Polish government, the directorate of Auschwitz Museum in 1994-- is presently on the outside wall of block 27, which is the block being dedicated to the Jewish martyrdom in Auschwitz. The translation of the plaque reads, 'For helping the prisoners of [NON-ENGLISH] commando in organizing an armed uprising in the camp against the SS. Following a torturing investigation on January 6, 1945, four Jewish heroic prisoners, participants of the underground in Auschwitz, were hanged. Ella Gertner, Roza Robota, Regina Safirsztain, Ester Wajcblum-- to hold their memory. National Museum of Auschwitz.'

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of the exhibit in Auschwitz in block 27 of Roza Robota, Ella Gertner, and Estusha Wajcblum. Unfortunately, no picture of Regina Safirsztain is available.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is this a reproduction of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a reproduction of an article in the Ottawa Jewish Bulletin in December 1994, which covers the ceremony of the plaque in Auschwitz. In this article, there is a plea to the Polish government to award the Medal of Honor to the three women as Roza Robota was awarded. To Ella Gertner, to Regina Safirsztain, and to Estusha Wajcblum. 'The Polish government has acknowledged the accuracy of the historical fact and is bestowing posthumously a Medal of Honor to Ella Gertner, Regina Safirsztain, and Ester Wajcblum.' On March 21st, in Polish embassy in Ottawa, Canada, I'm going to be honored, to be the recipient of the medal on behalf of my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a hand-carved box manufactured in my father's factory, named [NON-ENGLISH], in Poland before the war. I don't know when.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a matchbox hand-carved in the form of a hand. Hand-carved in my father's factory in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Around what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know-- before the Second World War. And those are the only two things that I have left from the factory. This is my husband and partner, my inspiration for the last 49 years, and I hope for many years to come. Joshua Heilman.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
